videos.description:
Discover the timeless magic of Venice with our exclusive video that takes you through the most enchanting places in the floating city. 

Start your virtual journey from St. Mark's Square, the pulsating heart of Venice, where history and art blend in timeless harmony. 

Continue your visual tour by crossing the famous Rialto Bridge and be captivated by the shimmer of the Grand Canal under the golden sunset light.
This video is your ideal travel guide if you are planning to visit Venice for a day or two. 

You will explore the hidden beauties of this unique city, from the vibrant islands of Murano and Burano to the majestic Doge's Palace. 
Every corner of Venice is a masterpiece waiting to be discovered, whether you choose to explore Venice on foot or glide along its iconic canals in a gondola. 

Immerse yourself in the unique atmosphere of Venice with our 4K video, capturing the essence of the city with stunning visual quality. 
From the heights of St. Mark's Campanile, you'll enjoy a breathtaking panoramic view. 

Discover what to see and do in Venice with our expert tips, from visiting the historic Acqua Alta Bookshop to exploring the most fascinating points of interest during your journey. 

If you're looking for an activity to do in these destinations, or you are seeking something customized like tours, excursions, guided visits, useful tips, etc., you can book your experiences through this link, receive a discount, and help this channel grow: getyourguide.it?partner_id=GKISS7X&utm_medium=online_publisher

videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Venice, and in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here as well as some essential travel tips to help you plan your trip. Let's hope you enjoy it! Venice is located in northeastern Italy. Nicknamed the "City of Canals", Venice was built on over a 100 islands and is connected by a network of bridges and walkways. In fact, the main tourist area is totally car-free, which makes it perfect for strolling around and soaking in the atmosphere. Beyond its romantic reputation, Venice boasts stunning architecture, a rich history, and cultural treasures. In this video, we highlight 10 of the best things to do in Venice, including some hidden gems. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures! Gliding through Venice's canals in a gondola is undeniably the most touristy experience in the city. Yet, for for many people, ourselves included, it's a bucket list item. Despite the cost, there is a romantic charm to cruising through the hidden waterways and under ancient bridges. In general, gondola rides last about 30 minutes and you can book a ride in person at the canals or pre-book it online to guarantee a spot. Teatro la Fenice rises from the city's canals in the San Marco District. This theater is one of Italy's most renowned opera houses and it was nicknamed "The Phoenix" since it rose from the ashes of fires three different times. The theater is open to visitors on a daily basis and includes an audio guide that tells the venue's rich history and significant role in Italian music. Venice is a photographer's paradise thanks to its many canals, which are adorned with iconic bridges. Spanning the grand canal, the Rialto Bridge is the most popular one and boast some stunning views. However, it does get crowded since it's on a well-used tourist path. For a touch of romance, admire the historic Bridge of Sighs. We'll talk more about the interesting story of this bridge later in the video. And the two other famous bridges that you can't miss are Constitution Bridge and Accademia Bridge, both offering great views of the sparkling canals. The famous bridges, they're beautiful, but they also do get very crowded. So if you want to find yourself a quieter area, come to Dorsoduro because the views and bridges here are equally as pretty. Getting lost on your own in Venice's winding alleys is charming, but a guided walking tour unlocks the city's intriguing history and stories. We joined a 2-hour walking tour, which started in the quieter Dorsoduro district. From there, we walked through tucked away alleys and past many historic landmarks on our way to the Rialto Bridge. The tour ended at St. Mark's Square, Venice's most famous attraction. Doing this walking tour on our first day set the perfect foundation for exploring the rest of Venice, since our guide also gave us a lot of local tips. Venice has many different islands that are waiting to be explored, with Burano and Murano being the most beautiful ones. Murano is a short boat ride away from the main tourist area and is famous for its glass blowing tradition that dates back centuries. You should definitely check out the glass blowing, but leave time to stroll around the picturesque canals here before heading to the next island. After another 30 minutes on the boat, you'll get to Burano. This was our favorite island of the two as it was filled with cozy restaurants and endless colorful buildings. There are over 20 million annual visitors in Venice, so it's very important that you book everything well in advance. We booked all our activities for this trip through GetYourGuide, from skip-the-line tickets to unique experiences, like the one we're doing right now, which brings us to the sponsor of today's video. GetYourGuide is the leading online travel experience platform and they offer more than 110,000 curated things to do in destinations all around the world. Venice has tons of unforgettable experiences to pick from. All of these are rated and reviewed by verified travelers just like us. This means you can find activities that match your interests and book with confidence. Their user-friendly App keeps everything organized too, with food tours, walking tours, and attraction tickets all in one convenient place. Plus, with skip-the-line access for popular attractions like the Doge's Palace, you can bypass the long lines where people are buying tickets and maximize your sightseeing time. What we also really like is GetYourGuide's 24/7 customer support and flexible cancellation policy. Overall, we've enjoyed using GetYourGuide during all of our travels and highly recommend them. Venice's beating heart lies in Piazza San Marco, a grand square framed by architectural marvels. Dominating the scene is the iconic San Marco Basilica, with its soaring domes and breathtaking golden mosaics. Inside, the ceilings are adorned with even more beautiful mosaics depicting biblical stories. Keep in mind that San Marco Square and Basilica are the busiest places in Venice, and you can expect lots of crowds during the day. For the best experience, book your Basilica entrance tickets online in advance, so you can skip the long waiting line. Standing shoulder-to-shoulder with St. Mark's Basilica, the Doge's Palace is another popular attraction. Built in the 14th century, this palace was the heart of the Venetian government. The Doge and his advisers conducted their affairs here, making crucial decisions that shaped Venice. Today, it's a museum where you can explore its opulent chambers adorned with frescoes. For us, the highlight was walking across the Bridge of Sighs, which served as a connecting passage between the Doge's Palace and the Prison. In fact, it's said that prisoners would let out a sigh here as they caught one last glimpse of their beautiful Venice before being taken to their cell. Italian cuisine is known for its fresh regional ingredients and delicious simplicity. However, Venice boasts a unique spin on Italian food. Seafood takes center stage here, with dishes like black squid ink risotto and creamy cod mousse. But Venice is also known for cicchetti, which are bite-sized delights similar to tapas. You'll also find a variety of regional wines here, like Prosecco and Bardolino. We indulged in the unique Venetian cuisine on a food tour. During this immersive tour, we visited local neighborhoods and wine bars in which we tried a variety of dishes and drinks. Venice's charm goes far beyond the touristic zone of San Marco. We highly recommend escaping the crowds and exploring Venice's quieter districts. Each district offers a unique flavor of Venice. Dorsoduro is the artistic zone, where many museums are located as well as picturesque canals. Castello is the quietest district, where you can find gardens and a historic shipyard. Finally, Cannaregio was our favorite area, boasting a local vibe with many authentic chicchetti and bacarí. There are great spots here to have a romantic dinner on the canal. Seeing Venice from above is a magical experience since you can see its winding canals, shimmering lagoon, and terracotta roofs. We took an elevator to the top of the iconic St Mark's Bell Tower, which is the highest point in Venice. From here, we had amazing 360-views over the city. Another more hidden gem option is the Palazzo Contarini del Bovolo, which is famous for its spiral staircase that leads to an observation terrace. While it's not as high as the Campanile, it still provides a unique viewpoint of the terracotta roofs and has fewer crowds. As we promised at the beginning of the video, we'll now share a few travel tips for Venice. First Venice's charm comes at a price, especially for accommodations in the heart of the city. Opt to stay out of the tourist zone for better prices. For example, we stayed in Mestre on the mainland of Venice, which is connected to the center by a short train ride or bus. Trains and buses run almost every 10 to 15 minutes, making it a convenient and budget-friendly alternative. Since the main island is car-free, Venice is best discovered on foot. Keep in mind that there are lots of bridges with stairs, making it challenging for strollers and wheelchairs. For those with accessibility concerns, water taxis or water buses are available. The water buses are also called "vaporetti" and these can get crowded, especially on peak times or on popular routes. However, they are the cheapest option. Water taxis are significantly more expensive than water buses and require some bargaining. Venice attracts large crowds, especially during public holidays, special events, and during weekends. The streets are narrow, so it can be hard to avoid people in the touristic areas. Booking activities and popular attractions well in advance is crucial to avoid disappointment. Consider visiting during weekdays or shoulder seasons in the spring or fall for a more relaxed experience. Finally, you may have heard of a fee to enter Venice. When we were there, this was in its trial period and cost 5€ per day. This applies only on certain days like weekends or public holidays for day trippers or travelers staying outside of Venice. Since this is a new thing, check the website before visiting to be updated about the latest restrictions. If you're if you're planning your Italy trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below the video. We also included other important links in the description that will help you with your Venice experience, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place that we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

videos.transcript: The city of Venice, easily one of the most beautifully unique in Europe, has been on our couple wish list for years and should probably be on yours, too. We did slightly lower our expectations for fear of scenes like this, which we will get to, but for any downsides Venice has, it's hard to dwell when its upside frequently looks like this. So, for this video, we'll share which ticketed attractions might work best for you, what expectations you might want to set if you're splashing out on a gondola, why its most popular day trip was a surprising highlight, some fantastic Italian food, of course, and to what extent do its busy streets ruin the fairy tale vibe. vibe. If you're new here, then welcome. We make travel movies from around the world, hoping you might just find your next holiday inspiration. This is Suitcase Monkey with a 3-day weekend in Venice. We arrived late last night. With the streets being peacefully quiet, it left us kind of in the dark about our choice of accommodation and neighborhood. So, it was somewhat of a pleasant surprise that with a bright September sun racing through the windows. This first wakeup was lovely. Our hotel was a handful of rooms set within the first floor of a traditional 1600's Venetian building, characterized by its high ceilings and stone balcony. And it was this latter part that was easily our highlight, perfect for each morning that was still to come. As usual, all restaurants, tours, our hotel, and more are linked in one free downloadable PDF guide for those wanting to replicate any part of this trip. We were staying in Canario, the northernmost district and historic Jewish quarter within central Venice. Throughout our time here, this area did feel pretty homely, not just because we slept here, but also felt relatively more lived in by its locals, especially along this one stretch with bars and restaurants creating an enjoyable nightly stroll. Even at 9:00 a.m. though, on our first morning, the city still felt like it was waking up. So, doing so even earlier is the best time to make the city feel like your own. The simple absence of cars maintains a timeless feel where even the mundane feels refreshing. Boats trying to navigate the tight waterways, deliveries inspired by Fred Flintstone, and even just waiting cargo. They all hit different here and make up a sizable part of the city's charm. As we made our way to the iconic St. Mark Square, the crowds obviously began to grow. This space and the buildings connected to it were once at the heart of politics, religion, and influence for the now dissolved Republic of Venice. But even today, its crowd-pulling abilities are still mitigated by its size. As one of the only wide open spaces here, it makes for a refreshing change, and there's plenty to experience. Numerous museums plotting its history, the Campanille bell tower, the famous bridge of size just around the corner. A €16 coffee with a €7 live music supplement fee, which if it's played to you by Vivaldi, is a really great deal. The striking Basilica of St. Marks, and of course, the Doge's Palace. For these last two, we actually booked a guided tour to get the full story. Once home to the city's highest elected power, the Doj's Palace is a grand building that takes an hour or two to explore. Made up with a unique mix of elaborate government offices, courtrooms, residential halls, and this grand chamber for council. Once the largest room in Europe, the tour mostly covers the republic's heyday until Napoleon's brief occupation in 1797. You also get to walk across the iconic bridge of size, which from the exterior is a popular photo stop. But when traversed from the inside, it's actually just a tiny cramped corridor connecting the palace to its jail. Its name is said to have come from the prisoners sighing as they looked out at their last sight of freedom before sentencing. Kind of like how I feel before I boarded Ryionaire. Attached to the palace and ending our tour was St. Marks Basilica, a 1,000-year-old cathedral that isn't just a place of worship, but a mini museum, too. Housing a collection of historic gems. But for me, the highlight was its 4,000 square m of walls covered with gold mosaic tiles stretching across a massive canvas. It is stunning in its own right. But it wasn't until I realized that this was all just made up of individual tiles that made it even more impressive. Lastly, we finished out on the terrace, offering sweeping views of the square below. It should be said that these two attractions should 100% be booked in advance, as the wait times can get crazy. So, I'll link more info in that PDF guide. As typical, I will also follow up this video with a more practical guide about visiting Venice, covering transport, costs, and ticketing options. So, once it's ready, I'll link that at the end. Our last stop around St. Mark Square was the Corore Museum, which was included with our ticket to the Doj's Palace. Focusing on the art and civilization of Venice, it spans 1,000 years of history with a range of galleries, artifacts, and decoration ideas for your kitchen. It definitely isn't an essential visit, but if you have the ticket already from visiting the palace, it's at least worth an hour of your time. Whilst 2 days in Venice gives enough time to see most of its highlights, having that extra 24 hours allows more of its best free activity. Since there are more side quests here than The Witcher 3, aimlessly wandering its streets is always a gift. So whilst we had a general destination in mind, we let the next few hours be guided by whatever caught our eyes. But no matter where you end up, at some point you will come across the Grand Canal. Running almost 4 km, it breaks its busiest islands in two and is a sight in itself. So much so, Venice is one of those rare cities where taking a trip on its public transport can be an attraction on its own. Its most famous landmark, though, is the unmistakable Rialto Bridge. Dating back more than 400 years, this marks the Grand Canal's oldest crossing, but is best experienced in the morning. And that's because at 9:30, it looked like this, and 5 hours later, it looked like this. To be fair though, the Rialto Bridge and its connecting streets were the only time that crowds ever actually impacted our time in Venice. So, it should be noted that for the rest of this trip, sure, the streets were busy at times, but never enough to have a meaningful negative impact. So, ultimately, this turned out to be a concern we needn't have worried about, at least not when visiting in late September. We ended our first day at this cozy little restaurant, largely offering seafood and pasta about 10 minutes from the Rialto Bridge. After wondering if I'd been given enough pepper, our first evening meal was a solid choice to end day one. But the best was still yet to come. Tomorrow, we'd be taking things a little slower, still on the hunt for our favorite Italian dish, exploring the city's most stunning opera house, and testing whether an iconic gondola ride is actually worth your money. But before we begin our second day in Venice, I want to rewind to our arrival. Nowadays, when booking a holiday anywhere, the large upfront payment of the flight itself can put pressures on your spending. So, for the times you're unable to commit to such a large cost so far in advance, I wanted to put pay later travel, the sponsor for this portion of the video, as a great way to get you where you want to go whilst managing your holiday budget. Pay later travel lets you find your preferred flight as you would normally, but then both lock in the price and save a seat for you with whatever deposit suits you best. This gives you the peace of mind against airline price hikes and availability, whilst breaking down the cost to a more manageable payment schedule over the weeks or months before takeoff. The total price shown to you at the time of booking is the final price, so there is no added interest from what you originally commit to. Once the final payment is made, your ticket will be sent to you as normal, and you can enjoy your holiday having paid everything off already. There are no credit checks, no impact on your credit score, no early repayment fees, and an excellent Trustpilot score from thousands of customer reviews. To learn more, visit pay later.com using code suitcase to get a $25 discount off your flight booking and lock in your next holiday. Venice and Gondelas are as synonymous with each other as Disneyland and Splash Mountain. Both are enjoyed on water. Both are a pleasurable journey at heart despite their commercialized nature. And both feel like something you probably should be doing just because, well, everyone else seems to be doing it. But despite what the 1980s Cornetto advert had me believe, only one of them guarantees someone singing to you. To be honest, we went into this with fairly low expectations, more so doing it for the channel. For walkups, the jetties are spread throughout the city and are hard to miss. The prices though are all standardized, so a private gondola for up to five people is a fixed €9 before 7:00 p.m. and €110 after. So, especially with a family, it works out less than I assumed for such a popular tourist attraction and much cheaper than say a shared ticket on the London Eye, which also lasts half an hour and dare I say it is less special than a private gondola ride in Venice. So, although it definitely isn't cheap, it may still be worth a punt price-wise for a special occasion. But, I must say most of our negative assumptions were only contained within the first few minutes. The gondilas hadn't had time to spread out and you can feel like just another customer. Pushing these valid issues aside, over the next 25 minutes, as we settled in and found more of our own space, we kind of got caught up in the moment. Watching the craft of the gondelers navigating the bridges and turns was one thing, but it does give you a whole new perspective to the city and turned into a surprising highlight. Heat. Heat. Heat. Heat. The remainder of our afternoon was a mix of restful indulgence, more joyful wandering of endless alleyways, and admiring one of Italy's most renowned theaters, and the city's most premier opera house. Not an opera fan myself, Maria Carlo, whose career was launched here, was my only recognizable touch point. But even so, the building itself, originally built in 1792, is stunning to walk through, and the 45-minute tour, is told well through its audio guide. As it turned out, today was also our best with food. For lunch, we love these small nibbles known as chiceti. They're a popular menu choice all over Venice, providing a quick, varied, tasty snack, and can often be found just by searching the word on Google Maps. Fans of the channel will also know that we love a good coffee stop. And so I'd really recommend this garden cafe, especially when you're as lucky as we were with the weather. Finally, later that evening, my favorite meal in Venice was at this really friendly restaurant where we enjoyed every single course. A smoked goose breast and tuna tatar for starter, a lovely pizza and seafood spaghetti for Maine, and a shared dessert. All going down like a treat. And so looking ahead to our final day tomorrow, for the first time, we'd be leaving its two main islands behind and heading northwards to check out its most popular day trip. Maybe to your surprise, Venice actually spreads over 400 km of lagoon and across more than 120 different islands. And so far on this trip, we had visited two. So, we thought the least we could do was see four. And that's why we booked this day trip to Morirano, famous for its worldrenowned glass making and Barano, known for its lace skills and brightly colored homes. As soon as we reached Morirano, it already felt like a different vibe. Even though it still is one of the more popular islands to visit, the feel was much slower, and our brief time wandering its streets was a welcome change of pace. But the highlight here was definitely watching the glass being manipulated into shape as part of our tour. This skill has been widespread all over Venice for more than 1,000 years. But in 1291, due to the large number of furnace fires, the Doge decided that all glass making should be moved away to Morirano Island, where I guess they'd be more understanding when they likely burnt down each other. There is, of course, a connected shop inside, which you can't film, but for expectations, it's best enjoyed more as an art gallery with beautifully impossible glass sculptures and less of a marketplace for your potential table wear, though there is still some of that, too. About 25 minutes further north by boat, Baranu Island was yet another key change and surprisingly became one of our high points for this whole holiday. We loved it here with its gorgeous main street peppered with shops, cafes, and restaurants. Outside of its main thoroughfare though, the eyelets here are largely residential. Home to almost 3,000 people, all the different shaded buildings you're seeing is said to have started right here. With its history as a fishing village, the story goes that with the island often covered in a thick morning fog, the different colors would help its fisherman signify whose home was whose. At least that's what Leonardo said to the maroon house lady after getting in bed with Burgundy. Although no one can be certain, it is believed that Barano's reputation as a master of lace also came from this same era with locals gaining relevant experience by repairing their own fishing nets. But it was at the end of the 16th century when Moroscina Morosini, the wife of the doge, stationed 130 lace makers on the island. And to this day, its expertise continues with items here made entirely by hand and often taking months to do so. It is unfortunately somewhat of a dying art, but added yet another color to our time in Barano. As mentioned, our next Venice follow-up video will cover more practical tips on what we would have done different for this trip in hindsight. More on using public transport and getting from the airport, which are the best and worst times to visit the city, costs, alternative hotel suggestions, and more. So, I'll link that video here once it's ready. Please leave a like, let me know any thoughts or questions you may have. Subscribe for more travel inspiration. And as ever, thank you for continuing to watch Suitcase Monkey.
